claim_text: Chlamydia trachomatis is most prevalent in the UK among sexually-experienced individuals aged 16 to 24.

runs.response_text:
VERDICT: SUPPORT

The Natsal-3 abstract directly supports this claim. It states that among sexually-experienced individuals (those who reported at least one sexual partner), Chlamydia trachomatis prevalence in the 16-24 age group was 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men, which were substantially higher than the overall prevalence of 1.5% in women and 1.1% in men across all ages tested (16-44 years). This demonstrates that Chlamydia is indeed most prevalent in the younger 16-24 age group within the UK p

VERDICT: SUPPORT  
The first abstract provides evidence that Chlamydia prevalence in individuals aged 16-24 years is relatively high, specifically stating rates of 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men within this age group, thus supporting the claim.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
Abstract [doc_id 5850219] states that chlamydia prevalence was 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men aged 16-24 years, which is higher than the overall prevalence in the surveyed population.

VERDICT: SUPPORT

The Natsal-3 study found chlamydia prevalence of 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men aged 16-24 years, which was higher than the overall prevalences of 1.5% and 1.1% respectively, directly supporting that chlamydia is most prevalent among sexually-experienced 16-24 year olds in Britain (UK).

VERDICT: SUPPORT
Abstract [doc_id 5850219] states that Chlamydia prevalence in individuals aged 16-24 years was 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men, compared to overall prevalences of 1.5% in women and 1.1% in men, indicating higher prevalence in this age group.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract from the Natsal-3 study reports chlamydia prevalences in individuals aged 16-24 years as 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men within Britain, and identifies this age group and sexual activity as risk factors.

VERDICT: SUPPORT

The first abstract from the Natsal-3 survey provides age-specific prevalence data for Chlamydia trachomatis, stating that prevalences in individuals aged 16-24 years were 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men. These rates are higher than the overall prevalences reported for women and men of all ages, confirming the claim.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract from the National Surveys of Sexual Attitudes and Lifestyles (Natsal-3) provides direct evidence that Chlamydia trachomatis prevalence is higher in sexually-experienced individuals aged 16-24, with rates of 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men in this age group.
